pages.title: Welcome to Vendor Invoice and Email Fraud
pages.subtitle: Learn how cybercriminals use fake emails, impersonation, and payment scams to target small businesses.

video.caption: Introduction to vendor invoice and email fraud (replace later)

pages.title: Why this is a cybersecurity issue
pages.text: Vendor invoice fraud is not just an accounting problem. It is often a cybersecurity issue because attackers use email compromise, fake domains, impersonation, or stolen accounts to trick businesses into sending money to the wrong place. A criminal may pretend to be a trusted vendor, office manager, or executive and ask for a payment update, urgent wire transfer, or new banking instructions. If an employee trusts the message without verifying it, the business may lose money, expose sensitive data, or create a larger security incident.

video.caption: How invoice fraud connects to cybersecurity

image.alt: Example of fraudulent vendor payment email (replace later)

pages.title: How vendor invoice fraud usually works
pages.text:
Many payment fraud attempts follow the same pattern.

A scammer sends an email that appears to come from a real vendor or internal employee. The message may include a fake invoice, a request to change bank details, or instructions to send payment quickly.

Sometimes the attacker uses a look-alike email domain. In other cases, they may have access to a real account and continue an existing email conversation.

The goal is to make the request seem normal enough that someone processes it without stopping to verify.

video.caption: Common steps in vendor payment fraud

image.alt: Illustration of vendor invoice fraud process

bullets.title: Attackers often target finance and operations staff
bullets.text: Anyone who handles invoices, payroll, purchasing, or vendor communication may be targeted.

bullets.title: A familiar name does not mean a message is safe
bullets.text: Scammers can spoof display names or use email addresses that look nearly identical to real ones.

bullets.title: Urgency is a common tactic
bullets.text: Messages may say the payment is overdue, confidential, or needs to be handled immediately.

bullets.title: Fraud can happen without malware
bullets.text: Some attacks succeed simply by manipulating trust, email habits, and weak verification processes.

image.alt: Did you know vendor fraud graphic (replace later)

pages.title: Red flags to watch for
pages.text:
Be extra cautious when a message involves money, account changes, or urgency.

Common warning signs include:

• A request to change payment or banking information
• Pressure to act quickly or keep the request secret
• An email address that is slightly different from the real vendor
• New payment instructions sent only by email
• A sudden change in tone, wording, or formatting
• Unexpected attachments or invoice files

Even if the request looks routine, verify it before processing payment.

video.caption: Example of a small business targeted by invoice fraud (replace later)

pages.title: How to respond safely
pages.text: If you receive a message about invoices, payment changes, or urgent transfers: 1) Pause before acting 2) Do not rely on email alone for payment changes 3) Verify through a trusted method, such as a known phone number or approved vendor contact 4) Report suspicious messages to your manager, finance lead, IT, or security contact 5) Do not send payment until the request is confirmed A simple verification step can prevent major financial loss.
